notes: Local school districts are primary authorizers; if denied, applicant may appeal to Commissioner of Education (state_authorizer_exists reflects this appeal pathway). law_last_amended_year and renewal_term_years (3 years) are approximate — verify against current Alaska statutes.

Original law included an annual cap of 10 startup schools per year for the first 5 years post-enactment (~2020 expiration); state_cap_exists reflects current post-sunset status. Alabama Public Charter School Commission can hear appeals from applicants denied by local district. law_last_amended_year is approximate.

Charter Authorizing Panel established 2013 as primary state authorizer. Original caps on open-enrollment schools effectively removed by 2011 amendment (auto-expanding cap). State authorizer can approve without local district consent.

ARS 15-183 grants charters for up to 15 years (initial and renewal). AZ State Board for Charter Schools (ASBCS) is the primary active authorizer; AZ State Board of Education has a self-imposed moratorium on new authorizations but ASBCS is unaffected. Universities under AZ Board of Regents and community college districts (>15k FTE) may also authorize. law_last_amended_year is approximate.

AB 1505 and AB 1507 (2019) significantly strengthened district denial authority, requiring finding that a charter would not be detrimental to the district. Appeals pathway: district → county board → State Board. NAPCS 4th edition ranks CA in national top 10. Practical entry has become harder since 2019 reforms.

Colorado Charter School Institute (CSI) is the state authorizer and can authorize independently of local districts. Districts can deny but applicants may appeal to State Board. NAPCS ranks CO #4 nationally. law_last_amended_year is approximate.

State Board of Education authorizes 'state charter schools' (independent LEAs, ~23 operating as of 2024-25). Local districts authorize 'local charter schools.' Individual school enrollment capped at 250 students (300 for K-8) or 25% of district enrollment, whichever is less. No hard statewide cap on number of schools. law_last_amended_year is approximate.

Delaware DOE with State Board assent is the primary authorizer. Districts may also authorize. Charter terms expire after 5 fiscal years; subsequent renewals every 5 years. law_last_amended_year is approximate.

Local school districts are primary authorizers; Charter School Appeal Commission (CSAC) can reverse district denials. No independent state authorizer — CSAC is an appellate body, not a proactive authorizer. Standard 5-year renewal; high-performing schools (A or B grade, sound finances) eligible for 15-year automatic renewal. HB 1285 (2024) expanded locations where private and charter schools can open. NAPCS ranks FL #5.

State Charter Schools Commission (SCSC) re-established by HB 797 after Nov 2012 constitutional amendment; prior 2008 commission struck down by GA Supreme Court in 2011. SCSC can authorize independently of local districts. Local districts also serve as authorizers.

2012 law eliminated cap and established State Public Charter School Commission as primary authorizer. 2012 law also permits accredited postsecondary institutions, county/state agencies, and nonprofits to apply to State Board to become authorizers (university_authorizer_exists reflects this possibility). NAPCS ranked HI 14th after recent reforms, jumping from 35th. Single-state DOE model limits authorizer diversity.

Iowa Code 256E (2021) replaced prior charter framework. Iowa State Board of Education is the primary authorizer; University of Northern Iowa was added as co-authorizer in subsequent legislation. Local school boards can initiate applications but the State Board makes authorization decisions. A 2026 law (May 2026) further expanded charter school access — details pending verification against enacted text.

Public Charter School Commission serves as state authorizer. Original school-count cap was removed. NAPCS cited Idaho as one of the states that lifted caps in recent reforms. law_last_amended_year is approximate.

Statewide cap of 120 charter schools (75 in Chicago, 45 in rest of state). Local districts are primary authorizers. Illinois State Charter School Commission can authorize on appeal when district denies. Sector is near the cap ceiling, particularly in Chicago. law_last_amended_year is approximate.

Multiple authorizer types: Mayor of Indianapolis, state board, Indiana DOE, local school corporations, and public universities (including Ball State). NAPCS ranks IN #9 nationally. law_last_amended_year is approximate.
Local school board is the sole authorizer with no state-level alternative or appeal pathway. NACSA rates KS among the lowest-ranked states for authorizer policy. law_last_amended_year is approximate.

No charter law. KY Supreme Court unanimously struck down HB 9 (2022 charter funding law) on Feb 19, 2026, ruling it unconstitutional — charter schools as structured fall outside KY's constitutionally defined 'common school' system. No charter schools ever opened in Kentucky. Future legislation would require voter approval of dedicated funding. Source: Kentucky Lantern, Feb 19, 2026.

Six charter school types (Type 1–5 + 3B): Types 2, 4, 5 authorized by BESE (state); Types 1, 3, 3B authorized by local districts. Major expansion post-Hurricane Katrina transformed New Orleans charter sector. NAPCS ranks LA #6. High-performing schools automatically renewed after 3 consecutive years of meeting goals. law_last_amended_year is approximate.

Cap of 120 charters (72 Commonwealth + 48 Horace Mann). Commonwealth charters are independent LEAs authorized by state DESE/Board; Horace Mann charters operate within a local district with union agreement required. Also subject to a 4% cap on district enrollment going to charters. Cap has been a major constraint on sector growth.

Local school board is the sole authorizer; no state-level authorization or appeal pathway. Charter teachers are subject to collective bargaining agreements. Very few charter schools operate. law_last_amended_year is approximate.

Hard statewide cap of 10 charter schools severely limits sector scale despite high law quality. Maine Charter School Commission is the primary state authorizer. Renewal standard 5 years; authorizer may grant up to 15-year renewal. NAPCS 4th edition ranks ME #2 nationally for law quality. law_last_amended_year is approximate.

Multiple authorizer types: public universities (Central Michigan, Grand Valley State, etc.), community college districts, intermediate school districts (ISDs), and local boards. University-authorizer cap of 150 schools expired December 31, 2014 — no statewide cap currently. Initial charter term up to 10 years with mandatory review at year 7.

First state to authorize charter schools (1991). Diverse authorizer ('sponsor') model: approved nonprofits, school districts, MN State system institutions (community colleges, universities), and others. State Board of Education must approve all sponsors. NAPCS ranks MN #1 nationally. 2024 amendments updated several charter provisions.

Statewide cap removed ~2021 (NAPCS cited Missouri as one of states that lifted its cap). State Board of Education and 4-year universities may authorize. Originally charter schools were limited to Kansas City and St. Louis; geographic restriction has been eased. law_last_amended_year approximate.

Mississippi Charter School Authorizer Board is the sole authorizer. Consent_required reflects § 37-28-7: in A-, B-, or C-rated districts the Authorizer Board may approve a charter only if the local school board endorses or initiates the application. D/F-rated districts require no local consent. Startup-only — conversions of existing public schools not permitted. HB 1432 (2025) amended the authorization statute.

Two laws signed May 2023: HB 549 (Public Charter Schools Act — local district initiates, Board of Public Education is final authorizer) and HB 562 (Community Choice Schools via new state commission). Parts of HB 562 were temporarily blocked by lawsuit as of September 2023. Montana is the 46th state to authorize charter schools. No charter schools operational as of research date. renewal_term_years is estimated — specific term not confirmed in HB 549 text searched.
NC State Board of Education is the sole authorizer. The 100-school statewide cap was removed in 2011. law_last_amended_year is approximate.

SB 2241 (2025) makes ND the 47th state to authorize charter schools. DPI (Department of Public Instruction) is the sole authorizer via performance agreements with the State Superintendent. No charter schools expected to open until fall 2027. Law requires licensed teachers, reducing autonomy relative to other new-law states. renewal_term_years not yet specified in statutory language searched.
No charter school law. EdWeek (August 2025) confirmed NE is one of three states without a charter law. No legislation pending as of research date.

No statewide cap on number of charter schools. State Board of Education can authorize directly without local board involvement; local authorization pathway also exists. A separate 10%-per-district student transfer cap applies unless waived by local board. ~37 charter schools operating as of 2024. law_last_amended_year is approximate.

NJ Commissioner of Education (NJDOE) is currently the sole authorizer. 2024 legislation proposed expanding authorizers to include IHEs and local boards but had not been fully enacted as of research date. No statewide numeric cap on number of schools; ~115 charter schools operating.
Public Education Commission (PEC) is the state authorizer; local districts may also authorize. NAPCS ranks NM #10 nationally.
Nevada State Public Charter School Authority (SPCSA) created 2017 as state-level authorizer alongside local district authorizers. No statewide cap.

Cap of 460 schools split 230 SUNY / 230 NYSED. Four authorizers: SUNY (university), NYSED Board of Regents (state), NYC DOE (local), Buffalo BoE (local). As of Oct 2025, ~370 charters authorized; NYC sector near its cap ceiling after latest approvals. NAPCS ranks NY #8. law_last_amended_year reflects 2015 cap expansion.

Multiple authorizer types: Ohio DOE, approved nonprofits (e.g., Thomas B. Fordham), school districts, educational service centers, and some universities. HB 2 (2015) significantly strengthened accountability and quality controls following sector-wide performance and oversight scandals. university_authorizer_exists is approximate — some university-affiliated entities have served as sponsors.
State Board of Education and local school districts may both authorize. law_last_amended_year is approximate.

Local school districts are primary authorizers (ORS 338). If district denies, applicant may appeal to OR State Board of Education or submit directly to an IHE (institution of higher education). IHE or State Board then becomes the authorizer if they approve. Initial charter term ≤5 years; first renewal same as initial; subsequent renewals 5–10 years. law_last_amended_year is approximate.

Local school districts are primary authorizers for brick-and-mortar charters; state Charter School Appeal Board (CSAB) handles denials but is an appeals body, not a proactive authorizer (state_authorizer_exists: false). Cyber charter schools are regulated separately. Districts retain strong practical denial authority. law_last_amended_year is approximate.
Hard statewide cap of 35 charter schools. Council on Elementary and Secondary Education (RIDE) is the sole authorizer. law_last_amended_year is approximate.

SC Public Charter School District (SCPCSD) is the primary state authorizer; local school districts and accredited IHEs (institutions of higher learning) may also authorize. 10-year renewal term upon renewal (§ 59-40-110). law_last_amended_year is approximate.
No charter school law. EdWeek (August 2025) confirmed SD as one of three states without a charter law.

Tennessee Public Charter School Commission (TPCSC) created in 2019 can override local board denials and become the authorizer if it finds denial contrary to students' best interests. Local boards remain authorizers in most cases. Renewal term is 10 academic years (TCA § 49-13-121). Achievement School District (ASD) authorizes in persistently low-performing schools.

Cap of 305 charter operators statewide (SB 2, 2013). TEA/State Board of Education is the primary state authorizer; 14 selected school districts may also authorize. TEA operates a competitive application cycle (Generation 28, 29, etc.). law_last_amended_year is approximate.
Utah State Charter School Board (USCSB) is the state authorizer; local school boards may also authorize. No statewide cap. law_last_amended_year is approximate.

Local school board is the sole authorizer and must approve; no state-level alternative or appeal pathway. VA Code § 22.1-212.12 sets renewal up to 5 school years. State Board of Education reviews applications but cannot override local boards. Very few charter schools operate in Virginia. law_last_amended_year is approximate.
No charter school law. EdWeek (August 2025) confirmed VT as one of three states without a charter law.

Original I-1240 (2012) struck down by WA Supreme Court in 2015 as violating public-school funding clause; SB 6194 (2016) replaced it under RCW 28A.710. Cap of 40 schools over a 5-year authorization window; window closed April 2021, so no new charter authorizations are possible under current law without legislative amendment. WA Charter School Commission is state authorizer; State Board may approve districts as local authorizers (initial district authorizing term 6 years). NAPCS ranks WA #3 nationally for law quality.

Multiple authorizer types: local school boards, UW System Office of Educational Opportunity (OEO), individual UW campus chancellors (UW-Milwaukee, UW-Parkside as legacy authorizers), City of Milwaukee common council, technical college district boards, tribal colleges, and others. Act 55 (2015) significantly expanded authorizer options beyond Milwaukee. No statewide cap.

Kanawha Circuit Court permanent injunction (Dec 2025) halting new charter schools without voter approval was paused by WV Supreme Court of Appeals in January 2026 (Case 25-851). Authorized schools continue operating while appeal is pending. Legal outcome unresolved as of research date — refresh this record when appeal concludes.

Local school board is the sole authorizer with no state-level alternative or appeal pathway. NACSA rates WY among lowest for authorizer policy. Very few charter schools operate. law_last_amended_year is approximate.
